items.title: Markets soar, oil prices plummet as hopes rise for U.S.-Iran peace
items.summary_auto: Oil prices fell under $100 US following pleas for peace in the Middle East by China's foreign minister, before rising a bit again. Markets in Europe and Asia soared at the same time, as did futures for major Wall Street indexes.

items.title: Canadian sues U.S. Homeland Security, which allegedly sought his Google data after critical social media posts
items.summary_auto: A Canadian is fighting back in U.S. federal court over what he says is an attempt by the Department of Homeland Security, through Google, to seek "vast swaths of information" about his personal life following social media posts critical of Donald Trump's administration.

items.title: B.C. residents baffled as strange shape floats through night sky
items.summary_auto: What was that in the night sky? It’s a question being asked by many residents in the B.C. Interior and north after an unusual sighting Tuesday night.

items.title: WHO confirms Andes strain of hantavirus in cruise ship passengers, with 3 transferred from ship for treatment
items.summary_auto: Health authorities have identified the Andes strain of hantavirus, which can be transmitted from person to person in passengers who were on a cruise ship at the centre of a deadly outbreak of the rare infection, officials said Wednesday.

items.title: Are teen social media bans a silver bullet?
items.summary_auto: Manitoba recently joined a growing list of places that have implemented, or are considering social media bans for people under the age of 16. Are bans a silver bullet?

items.title: 'The rats are like a storm': Palestinians seeking refuge in rubble struggle with rodent infestations
items.summary_auto: Palestinians living in tents amid the rubble in the Gaza Strip and aid groups trying to help them say rodent infestations have left them suffering from diseases and unsure how to cope.

items.title: Canada's government is looking at privatizing airports. What would that accomplish?
items.summary_auto: If Canada proceeds with privatizing airports, it could open opportunities for investors and infrastructure improvements. But it could also mean higher costs for air passengers.

items.title: Security protocol adopted after Hydro One hired alleged ISIS recruiter 'unreasonable': labour decision
items.summary_auto: After hiring an alleged ISIS recruiter, Ontario's largest electricity provider tried to bring in a much more intensive security screening process to protect its grid — but a labour arbitrator has found Hydro One's national security background checks were "intrusive" and "unreasonable."

items.title: Results of privacy investigation into OpenAI's ChatGPT coming today
items.summary_auto: The results of an investigation into whether OpenAI's ChatGPT adheres to Canada's privacy laws will be made public this morning.

items.title: Montrealers celebrate Canada's incoming GG Louise Arbour, who got her start in the city
items.summary_auto: Long before Rideau Hall was on the horizon, Louise Arbour's home was in Montreal, where her impact and influence is still felt today.

items.title: As drinking dries up, Quebec's liquor board faces criticism over efforts to boost sales
items.summary_auto: With the consumption of alcohol on a steady decline, the province’s state-run liquor retailer is making an aggressive attempt to make sure sales don’t dip. Public health experts question whether it's going too far.

items.title: Mexico moved extradition goalposts after U.S. indicted top politician over cartel ties: experts
items.summary_auto: The Claudia Sheinbaum administration's demand for "irrefutable" proof before handing over a top elected politician to the U.S. over alleged drug cartel dealings reveals how the Mexican government applies a double standard in its stated battle against organized crime, experts say.

items.title: Ford losing support in wake of jet controversy, polls suggest
items.summary_auto: Doug Ford’s now grounded plan to buy a private jet highlights a trend of softening support for the premier and his government, say some experts and polling analysts, who warn that the controversy could be a sign of deeper popular unease with the Tories’ agenda.

items.title: Quick service restaurants are taking a bigger hit as Canadians feel crunched by the cost of living
items.summary_auto: While restaurants of all kinds are feeling pinched, quick service restaurants are getting hit harder than higher-end ones, according to a report from Restaurants Canada. It's a signal of a  K-shaped economy , where those with the most money can still shell out for a nice meal, but those with lower incomes have no option but to cut back.

items.title: Canada to pick between Swedish and U.S. radar planes to protect its skies
items.summary_auto: The government of Canada wants to quickly acquire half a dozen new "flying radars" — officially called Airborne Early Warning and Control (AEW&C) aircraft — at a cost of more than $5 billion.

items.title: Marie-Philip Poulin adds to her legend, scores in 3OT to lift Victoire over Frost
items.summary_auto: Marie-Philip Poulin scored at 4:02 of the third overtime period and Ann-Renee Desbiens made 38 saves to record the shutout as the Montréal Victoire defeated the Minnesota Frost 1-0 in Game 2 of the PWHL semifinal series on Tuesday.

items.title: Telecom workers say AI being used to monitor employees, disguise accents of offshore agents
items.summary_auto: Telecommunications workers are calling for government restrictions on the use of artificial intelligence in the sector, suggesting the technology is being used to monitor workers and disguise the accents of overseas call centre workers.

items.title: U.S. rights group sues New York Times, claiming discrimination against a white man
items.summary_auto: A U.S. federal civil rights agency filed a discrimination lawsuit Tuesday against the New York Times, claiming that the news organization passed over a white male employee for a promotion in favour of a lesser qualified woman to meet its diversity goals.

items.title: Tumbler Ridge shooting survivor's 5th surgery 'a success,' father says
items.summary_auto: The father of Maya Gebala, the 12-year-old seriously injured during the mass shooting in Tumbler Ridge, B.C., says his daughter has undergone a successful cranioplasty.

items.title: Canada and Europe should get ‘creative’ in forging closer ties, EU ambassador says
items.summary_auto: The EU’s Ambassador to Canada, Geneviève Tuts, says the bloc is looking to "elevate" its relationship with this country and take it to the "next level."

items.title: Carney government 'not working' to enshrine nature targets into law, junior nature minister says
items.summary_auto: Despite the Carney government's parliamentary majority, Canada's junior nature minister said she has no plans to back up recent commitments to protect Canada's waters and lands in legislation.

items.title: Vancouver breaks temperature record last set in 1898 amid hot weather across B.C.
items.summary_auto: Environment Canada says Vancouver's high temperature on Monday reached 23.9 C, easily breaking the record of 22.2 C set in 1898.

items.title: 'A fortuitous bounce': Maple Leafs win NHL draft lottery, Canucks to pick 3rd
items.summary_auto: The Toronto Maple Leafs won the NHL draft lottery Tuesday, handing newly hired general manager John Chayka and incoming senior executive adviser of hockey operations Mats Sundin immediate control of the No. 1 pick less than a day after their introduction.

items.title: Canadiens vs. Sabres: What to know for NHL's best 2nd-round matchup
items.summary_auto: CBC Sports' daily newsletter previews the Montreal-Buffalo Stanley Cup playoff series, an exciting matchup between up-and-coming Atlantic Division rivals.

items.title: Trump pauses U.S. effort to guide vessels through Strait of Hormuz in bid to make deal with Iran
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump says that he has paused the U.S. effort to guide stranded vessels out of the Strait of Hormuz to finalize an Iran deal.

items.title: Prosecutors add assault charge to case against suspect in White House press gala shooting
items.summary_auto: The U.S. Justice Department on Tuesday added ‌a charge of assault on a federal officer with a deadly weapon to the case ​against the man accused of ​trying to assassinate President Donald Trump at the White House correspondents' ​dinner in Washington, D.C., last month.

items.title: He was teaching CPR, then went into cardiac arrest. His students saved him
items.summary_auto: Karl Arps was demonstrating the signs of a heart attack during a training course when he really had one, and went into cardiac arrest.

items.title: Photo of 6-fingered woman in PSA shows N.L. government needs to 'tighten up' AI policy: premier
items.summary_auto: Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Tony Wakeham says he will make sure policies around the use of artificial intelligence are enforced. This comes after an altered image was posted on government's Facebook page.

items.title: Human-to-human transmission of killer hantavirus suspected in cruise ship outbreak
items.summary_auto: After a spate of deaths and illnesses stemming from a hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship, health officials now suspect human-to-human transmission may have played a role in the spread of this potentially deadly infection. So what's the risk to broader public health?

items.title: Ontario lowers screening age for colorectal cancer to 45
items.summary_auto: New guidelines published online by Cancer Care Ontario say the province is lowering its colorectal cancer screening age from 50 to 45 in response to a "notable" increase in younger people diagnosed with the disease.

items.title: 14 celebrities who really went for the Met Gala red carpet theme 'Fashion is Art' this year
items.summary_auto: From classical sculpture to surrealist paintings, these designs brought art history to life .

items.title: Hit show Tracker on the run from Vancouver, production lured to California by tax credit
items.summary_auto: The production of the hit CBS TV series Tracker is making a run to California, after three seasons of filming throughout Vancouver and around southern British Columbia.

items.title: Sask. announces new requirements for carbon monoxide alarms after boy's death last year
items.summary_auto: "This law, Henry's Law, will help make sure that families and children are safe in their homes," said Marina Hills, the mother of Henry Losco.

items.title: Fact check: Nicki Minaj, Lady Gaga and other fake Met Gala photos
items.summary_auto: Which celebrity photos from the Met Gala are real, and which are fake? Several AI-generated photos and videos circulated online during this year’s event.

items.title: The Kremlin has been throttling the internet and blaming security threats. Many Russians aren't buying it
items.summary_auto: In Moscow, residents have experienced multiple internet outages recently, which have made it impossible to use the mobile web. The government says the measures are necessary for security, but many see it as part of the Kremlin's tightening grip on the country's digital space.

items.title: Top-ranked Aryna Sabalenka calls for boycott if tennis players don't get bigger cut of Grand Slam revenues
items.summary_auto: Top-ranked Aryna Sabalenka believes tennis players should organize a boycott if they don't start receiving a bigger share of tournament revenues at the Grand Slams. The likes of Coco Gauff, Elena Rybakina and Jasmine Paolini are prepared to protest, too.

items.title: Former governors general claimed $554K from expense account last year
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney says he will look into an expense program under which former governors general billed the government for more than half a million dollars last year. The program, which launched in 1979, has been shrouded in secrecy despite calls for greater transparency.

items.title: Judge grants stay of deportation to keep Mexican father with his young family in Montreal
items.summary_auto: A 22-year-old Mexican man living in Montreal has been granted a last-minute stay of deportation after a Federal Court judge ruled his removal could cause serious harm to his young family. Immigration advocates are warning of rising removals and tougher enforcement in Quebec.

items.title: Former police officer charged in fatal Toronto shooting
items.summary_auto: Michaelo Markicevic, 67, was arrested and charged with second-degree murder in the shooting death of Daniel Stopnicki, 47 in April. CBC News has confirmed that Markicevic is a former police officer with Collingwood police and former York University executive.

items.title: Researchers are giving salmon cocaine. Don't worry, it's for science
items.summary_auto: With cocaine and its metabolite, benzoylecgonine, being detected in waterways across the globe, Canadian researcher Erin McCallum and her team set out to understand the impact of these illicit drugs on fish in Sweden.
